body.p:
The superposition principle of states (see Chapter 14) applied to a composite system allows the existence of entangled states with no equivalent in classical physics. This concept was introduced by Schrödinger as early as 1935 and popularised by the thought experiment of ‘Schrödinger’s cat’.
1

The same year, Einstein, Boris Podolsky and Nathan Rosen put forward an epistemological argument claiming to reveal the incompleteness of the current quantum theory, now known as the ‘EPR argument’.
2
This argument was at the origin of numerous theoretical and experimental works that sought to provide scientific answers to the philosophical debate that had been raised.

sec.title: Entanglement in a Composite System
sec.p: Entanglement of states is a purely quantum concept, the consequences of which challenge the classical description of physical systems.

caption.p: (a) Separable and (b) entangled states in a quantum well.
